notes.section_title: Introduction: The Tapestry of the Past

content.text: History is more than just a collection of dates and events. It is an 'unending dialogue between the present and the past', as historian E.H. Carr said. We study history to understand how we arrived at our present world. Museums, like the National Museum in New Delhi, preserve objects from the past—such as statues, coins, and ornaments—which help us learn about our history.

notes.section_title: How Do We Learn About the Past?

content.text: Understanding the past requires the work of many different kinds of experts. Each of them studies a specific aspect of the past.

content.title: Key Experts and Their Roles
items: Geologists: Study the physical features of the Earth, like soil, stones, hills, mountains, rivers, and oceans.
Palaeontologists: Study the remains of plants, animals, and humans from millions of years ago in the form of fossils.
Anthropologists: Study human societies and cultures from the oldest times to the present.

Archaeologists: Study the past by digging up remains that people, plants, and animals left behind. This includes tools, pots, beads, figurines, toys, bones and teeth of humans and animals, burnt grains, and parts of houses or bricks.

notes.section_title: How Is Time Measured in History?

content.text: Different societies have their own ways of measuring time, often marking a new era with a major event like the birth of an important person or the start of a ruler's reign. Today, the Gregorian calendar is commonly used worldwide. The Gregorian calendar is a solar calendar with 12 months totalling 365 days, and a leap year every four years (except century years that are not multiples of 400).

In this calendar, the conventional year of Jesus Christ's birth is the starting point. Years after this are counted forward and were marked with 'AD' (Anno Domini, Latin for 'in the year of the Lord'). Today, they are more commonly called CE (Common Era). Years before this date are counted backwards and were marked with 'BC' (Before Christ). They are now called BCE (Before Common Era). For example, the year India gained independence, 1947, can be written as 1947 CE.

topics.topic_name: Calculating Time Across BCE and CE

content.title: Important Note on Calculations
content.text: There is no year 'zero' in the Gregorian calendar. The year 1 BCE is followed immediately by the year 1 CE. This means when calculating the number of years between a BCE date and a CE date, you must add them and subtract 1. For example, the number of years between 560 BCE (birth of the Buddha) and 2024 CE is 560 + 2024 - 1 = 2583 years.

A timeline is a convenient tool to mark events in a sequence. It shows a line from the past to the present with important events placed along it. Timelines help us understand the order in which historical events took place. For instance, by looking at a timeline, we can see that the birth of the Buddha occurred before the birth of Jesus.

content.definition: A period of 100 years. In history, specific centuries are counted every 100 years starting from the year 1 CE. For instance, we are currently in the 21st century CE, which runs from 2001 to 2100. Centuries BCE are counted backwards from 1 BCE, so the 3rd century BCE includes the years 300 BCE to 201 BCE.

A period of 1000 years. In history, specific millennia are counted every 1000 years from the year 1 CE. For instance, we are in the 3rd millennium CE (2001-3000 CE). The 1st millennium BCE includes the years 1 BCE to 1000 BCE.

notes.section_title: What Are the Sources of History?

content.text: A source of history is any place, person, text, or object from which we gather information about a past event or period. Historians and other experts use a variety of sources to piece together the story of the past, like putting together a jigsaw puzzle. Sometimes pieces match, and sometimes they give contradictory information, requiring experts to decide which source is more reliable.
Archaeological sources are the material remains of past human activity. These are often uncovered through excavations (digging) at sites like ancient mounds, habitations, and burials.

content.title: Types of Archaeological Sources
items: Artistic Sources: Paintings, sculptures, and panels.
Tools and Weapons: Stone axes, blades, arrowheads.
Pottery and Toys: Clay pots, figurines, and toys.
Ornaments: Beads made of stone or shell, pendants made of animal teeth.
Human, Animal, and Plant Remains: Bones, teeth, and burnt grains.

content.text: Literary sources are written texts that provide information about the past. They can be divided into two broad categories.

content.title: Types of Literary Sources
items: Indian Literature: Includes religious texts like the Vedas, epics like the Itihasas (Ramayana and Mahabharata), poems, plays, scientific and technological texts, historical chronicles, and collections of stories.
Foreign Accounts: Travelogues and historical accounts written by visitors from other lands. These offer an outsider's perspective on Indian society, culture, and politics.

content.text: Oral sources are traditions passed down through speech from one generation to the next. This includes folklore, songs, ballads, and genealogies (family trees). These sources can preserve memories and stories that were never written down.

The field of history is constantly enriched by new scientific methods. In the last 50 years, studies of ancient climates (palaeoclimatology), chemical analysis of excavated materials, and genetics (the study of how features are passed down through generations) have provided fresh insights that supplement traditional sources. For recent history (the last 200-300 years), sources like newspapers and, for the last few decades, electronic media (television, the internet) are also used.

Modern humans (Homo sapiens) have lived on Earth for about 300,000 years. Our early ancestors faced many challenges from nature and lived in bands or groups for survival. They were constantly on the move, seeking shelter and food. They were primarily hunter-gatherers, meaning they relied on hunting animals and gathering edible plants and fruits for sustenance.

They lived in temporary camps, rock shelters, and caves. They used fire and made simple tools like stone axes, blades, and arrowheads to make life easier. They also communicated using languages, though these are now lost to us. Aspects of their lives are visible in rock paintings found in hundreds of caves worldwide, which often depict animals and hunting scenes. Early humans also developed certain beliefs about nature and may have had some notion of an afterlife.

The Earth's climate has undergone many changes. The last Ice Age lasted from about 100,000 years ago to around 12,000 years ago. When the climate warmed, the ice melted, creating new rivers and improving living conditions.

This warming led to a major shift for humans. In many parts of the world, people began to settle down, cultivate cereals and grains (agriculture), and domesticate animals such as cattle and goats. This period marks the beginning of farming. With a more reliable food supply, communities grew in size and number and often settled near rivers. The soil near rivers was fertile, making farming easier. As settlements grew, social structures became more complex, with leaders or 'chieftains' emerging to look after the community's welfare.

Over time, small hamlets grew into sizeable villages. People began exchanging goods like food, cloth, and tools, establishing networks of communication and exchange. Some of these villages grew into small towns.

This period also saw new technologies. Pottery was developed for making clay pots and other objects. The use of metals began, first with copper and later with iron. These metals helped make durable tools, weapons, and ornaments. This stage of growth prepared the ground for the emergence of what is called 'civilisation', which will be explored in later chapters.

glossary.definition: The study of the human past. It is an 'unending dialogue' between the present and the past.
An expert who studies the past by excavating and analysing the material remains left behind by people, plants, and animals.
The preserved impression or remains of a plant, animal, or human from the ancient past, found within layers of soil or rock.
The internationally accepted civil calendar, with 12 months, 365 days, and a leap year every four years. It is based on the traditional year of the birth of Jesus.
CE stands for Common Era, used for years after the conventional date of Jesus' birth (replacing AD). BCE stands for Before Common Era, used for years before that date (replacing BC).
A period of one hundred years.
A period of one thousand years.
A graphical representation of a sequence of events in chronological order, used as a tool to understand the order and duration of historical periods.
Any place, person, text, or object from which we gather information about a past event or period.
Material remains of past human activity, such as monuments, tools, pottery, and bones, which are uncovered through excavation.
Written texts from the past, including religious books, poems, plays, historical chronicles, and accounts of foreign travellers.
Historical information passed down through generations by word of mouth, such as folklore, songs, and genealogies.
People who survive by hunting wild animals and gathering wild plants, fruits, and nuts rather than by farming or raising domesticated animals.
A long period in Earth's history when global temperatures were much colder, and large ice sheets covered vast areas of land.
The process of adapting wild plants and animals for human use, through selective breeding and care.

summary_points: History is studied by various experts, including archaeologists, anthropologists, and geologists, each using different methods to understand the past.
Time is measured in history using the Gregorian calendar, with dates categorised as BCE (Before Common Era) and CE (Common Era); there is no year zero.
A timeline is a crucial tool for placing events in chronological order, helping us understand when they happened in relation to each other.
Sources of history are broadly classified into archaeological (material remains) and literary (written texts), along with oral traditions and modern scientific aids like genetics.
Early humans were hunter-gatherers who used stone tools, lived in caves, and created rock art; a major shift occurred after the last Ice Age with the development of agriculture and settled life.
The shift to farming led to the growth of villages, the development of new technologies like pottery and metal use, and eventually the rise of towns, setting the stage for civilisation.
